title: Luhn-Valid BIN Range Enumeration with Sequential Card Testing
narrative: Fraudster generates algorithmically-valid card numbers within a single BIN range (first 6-8 digits), then tests them in rapid succession across multiple merchant IDs. A cybercriminal obtains BIN 442637 from a data breach, then uses a Luhn algorithm generator to create 200+ valid card number candidates. Within a 90-minute window, these cards attempt $1.00-$4.99 authorization checks across 3-5 different MCCs (typically digital goods MCC 5816, telecom MCC 4814). The sequential nature creates a distinctive staircase pattern: card1 values increment by 1-3 digits per transaction. All attempts share identical P_emaildomain (temp-mail.org or guerrillamail.com), same dist1 value indicating single device geolocation, and clustered txn_hour values (usually is_night=1). Successful authorizations immediately followed by larger transactions ($47-$299) within 2-8 minutes using the validated card numbers.
indicators: card1 values incrementing sequentially (Â±1-3 digits) across 40+ transactions in 90-minute window
Single BIN prefix (first 6-8 digits identical) across 85%+ of transaction batch
Initial transaction amounts $1.00-$4.99 followed by secondary charges $47-$299 within 2-8 minutes on same card1
Identical P_emaildomain across 95%+ of attempts, typically disposable email services
card1_txn_count_1hr exceeding 15 for newly-seen card numbers with card1_txn_count_lifetime=1
dist1 value consistent across batch indicating single device origin despite multiple card1 values
Concentration in is_night=1 hours (22:00-06:00) with 70%+ of volume in 3-hour window
summary: A single device tests 40+ card numbers from the same BIN prefix in quick succession, starting with small $1-5 authorization checks followed immediately by larger charges on successful cards. Sequential card numbers increment by just 1-3 digits, all using the same disposable email domain and originating from one location during nighttime hours.
reasoning: The attacker is manufacturing valid card numbers from a stolen BIN and systematically testing which ones work, using small amounts to avoid triggering fraud alerts before exploiting validated cards for higher-value purchases. The sequential pattern, single device origin, and disposable emails reveal organized enumeration rather than legitimate customers making independent purchases.
indicator_explanations: Card numbers are being tested in sequence, incrementing by small amounts
All cards share the same bank identification prefix during the attack
Small test charges are quickly followed by larger fraudulent purchases
Same throwaway email address is reused across nearly all transaction attempts
Brand-new card numbers are being hit with rapid-fire transaction bursts
All transactions originate from the same device despite changing card numbers
Attack is concentrated in overnight hours when monitoring is typically reduced
